ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hormones are a group of chemicals which play a significant role in regulating the natural state of the body. The natural sleep cycle, sexual function, reproduction, growth, and development are significant on them. In addition, they act as an agent to facilitate the communication between the body and brain as well as regulate the stress response and circadian rhythm. On the other hand, dysregulated and abnormal hormonal processes can lead to various diseases in the human body. Therefore, precise and prompt monitoring of hormones can be considered one of necessities of a healthy society. Recently, analytical approaches based on molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have revealed a high potential for sensitive and selective detection hormones. However, the lack of conductivity and electrocatalytic activity in MIPs can limit their applicability in the sensing field to some extent. Thereby, the incorporation of nanomaterials with MIPs can open exciting possibilities for the efficient detection of hormones. In this review, we have taken a closer look at the current research advances in MIP-based bio(sensors), highlighting and summarizing the key findings. Additionally, we will provide an overview of MIP modified with nanomaterials and biological elements for experimental identification and point-of-care testing of hormones. It should be noted that this is the first comprehensive review of the identification of hormones using MIP-based sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hormones, as messengers, can play important roles within the human body including regulation of sexual function, contribution to the immune system, growth and metabolism [1]. They can be classified based on multiple factors including their mechanism of action, physiological effects, endocrine stimulation patterns, chemical composition, and distribution within the body. On the basis of chemical nature, hormones are classified into three groups including amino acid derived hormones, steroid hormones and protein hormones [2]. These signaling molecules are produced by the adrenal glands, pituitary gland, thymus, pancreas, pineal and thyroid, all of which can be considered as principle of classification into two main groups consisting of endocrine and exocrine glands [3–6]. In terms of endocrine glands, hormones are released into the circulatory system, where they are transported throughout the body to interact with target organs and tissues [7]. Elaborately, the proper functioning of the endocrine system is essential for the regulation of sleep and wakefulness, as it involves the release of various hormones such as cortisol, melatonin, and growth hormones that influence physiological processes [8]. On the other hand, disruptions to the endocrine system, whether due to medical conditions or environmental factors, can cause negative consequences to sleep and overall health [9]. Furthermore, there exists compelling evidence supporting the relationship between hormones and the immune system and this, in turn, makes this topic more interesting [10]. Metabolism, the set of chemical processes that occur within a living organism to maintain life, is subject to another hormonal regulation in the human body [11]. To illustrate, key hormones involved in this regulation are testosterone and estrogen, both of which are known as metabolic homeostasis agents. In favor of estrogen, not only it can play a crucial role in mineral homeostasis, but it can also exert significant effects on the skeletal system [12]. Through its influence on bone metabolism, estrogen helps to maintain bone mass and density, as well as to promote bone growth and repair [13]. In terms of testosterone, not only it can be implicated in the aging process, but it can also be considered a key determinant of body composition in humans [14]. In addition to the hormones mentioned previously, several other hormones including insulin, cortisol, thyroid hormones, and progesterone play critical roles in human physiology. Insulin, for example, is a pancreatic hormone that regulates glucose metabolism and is necessary to maintain blood sugar levels within a narrow range. In addition, progesterone, a steroid hormone produced by the corpus luteum in the ovaries, is essential in regulating the menstrual cycle and maintaining pregnancy [15].

Over the few decades, numerous analytical approaches including radioimmunoassay (RIA) [16], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [17], enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) [18], chemiluminescent immunoassay (CLIA) [19], electrophoresis-electrospray ionization-mass spectrometry [20], ultra-filtrations [21], mass spectrometry (MS) [22] and high-performance liquid chromatography coupled mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [23] have been fabricated for the identification of hormones due to the importance of hormones in our lives. Despite the availability of a wide variety of sensing methods for hormones detection, only a limited number of methods are cost-effective, sensitive and reproducibility [24]. In many cases, these platforms are associated with certain limitations such as complicity and tedious processes, time-consuming detection, instability and the need for an expert and trained operator [25]. To overcome the limitations of existing hormone detection methods, researchers are dedicating significant efforts towards the development of new methods that offer quantitative analysis of hormones and provide various advantages over traditional methods. In this regard, biosensors are high potential alternative of conventional approaches [26]. To illustrate, easy-to-use tools such as optical [27] and electrochemical (EC) [28] bio(sensors) were developed for the detection of hormones in real samples. In recent years, the field of sensing and biosensing technology has gained considerable interest owing to its immense potential [29]. To elaborate, in order to detection hormones by biosensors, several their advantages including simplicity, affordability, sensitivity, stability, and accuracy can introduce direct, efficient and, real-time analytical approaches.

One of the inherent limitations of sensor-based platforms lies in their selectivity, which poses a significant challenge to their widespread application in the detection of various analytes in real samples [30]. Therefore, it is necessary to modify the sensors to increase their selectivity for the detection of target analytes [31]. Enzyme and antibody-based immunoassays are functional matrices that have been utilized for achieving specific recognition of target probes [32]. However, the limited chemical and physical stability of antibodies and enzymes limits their use in harsh environments including high temperature, organic solvents and acid or base [33]. Compared to enzymes and antibodies, MIPs offer several unique advantages, such as good stability, easy preparation, affordability and robustness against environmental changes which have led to the introduction of them as a suitable option for use in diagnostic sensors. To elaborate, many MIPs have good stability in aqueous environments, organic solvents, extreme pH conditions and temperature changes [34].

The utilization of nanomaterials has demonstrated a significant impact on various fields including medicine, energy conversion, sensors, and catalysts [35]. Recently, there has been growing interest in the specific properties of nanomaterials, especially in biosensing, owing to interesting properties including high surface area, controllable shape and proportions, and specific chemical and physical properties. In particular, using them alongside MIP increases the analytical performance of sensors by measuring signal transmission of biological phenomena [36].

As far as we know, there is no systematic and comprehensive review of MIP-based sensors for hormone sensing. Therefore, in this study, we tried to investigate the performance of biosensors based on MIPs in hormone monitoring. In line with hormone detection, the role of nanomaterials was highlighted along with MIPs. The primary objective of this study is to compare the various types of sensors and biosensors and identify the research and technology gaps in this field. To provide further clarification, this review will categorize the various methods EC, and optical techniques used for measuring hormones based on the MIPs and nanomaterials roles.

Molecularly imprinted technique (MIT), also known as molecular template technology, is an innovative approach for improving the sensitivity and selectivity of sensors through the development of surface chips [37]. This technique could offer precise identification of target molecules [38]. Molecularly imprinted technique provides a promising approach to designing smart polymeric networks with predetermined identification potential for specific biomolecule determination [39]. Molecularly imprinted polymers are generated through a copolymerization process of crosslinkers and functional monomers in the presence of template molecules. A suitable solvent is also used to dissolve monomers, cross-linkers and template molecules during the polymerization process [40]. The formation of this polymer is conducted by non-covalent, covalent, and hydrogen bonding interactions between the template molecule and functional monomers [41]. Following the removal of the template molecule from the MIP film, the resulting recognition sites exhibit a high degree of complementarity in terms of structure, size, shape, and functionality to the template analyte [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and limitations of the MIPs-based biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The specific recognition properties, chemical and physical stability of MIPs make them attractive for the identification and attachment of hormones [43]. To elaborate, the following advantages of MIPs can be categorized into four important parts. First, MIPs show high selectivity towards distinct target molecules, thereby enabling precise identification and quantification of different analytes. Second, the polymers under consideration exhibit notable resilience towards various environmental conditions, including pH levels, temperature fluctuations, and solvent composition. This remarkable attribute significantly enhances the dependability of sensors based on MIPs. Furthermore, by adjusting the polymerization conditions and choosing the appropriate functional monomer, MIPs can be designed to detect a wide range of analytes, so they have good versatility. Importantly, the cost of their synthesis and preparation is relatively low compared to other biological factors. Despite the significant benefits of MIPs-based sensors, there are several limitations that may restrict their practical utility in certain areas [44]. One of the main challenges is related to the accessibility of recognition sites, which can be hindered by the physical structure of the MIP [45]. This can result in a reduced binding capacity and sensitivity towards the target analyte. The limitations of MIPs-based sensors are due to the nature of the synthesis method, which involves the complete insertion of the binding site and template into the polymer matrix [46]. To address the limitations of molecularly imprinted-based sensors, several alternative strategies have been proposed, one of which is the fabrication of MIP films at the surface of solid supports, additionally, the use of core-shell imprinted nanoparticles. The location of imprinting sites within the polymer matrix can have a significant impact on the recognition properties of MIPs [47]. In addition, the integration of molecularly imprinted sensors with nanomaterials provides another strategy to increase the number of printed sites on the chip surface and enhance the performance of MIP-based sensors [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical bio(sensors) based on MIPs for hormones detection
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical analysis presents a feasible and economical technique for detecting the presence and concentration of electroactive species in a given solution. In other words, this method brings about both qualitative and quantitative analysis. The fundamental principle underlying the operation of EC biosensors is the occurrence of oxidation-reduction reactions that conduct between the analyte molecules and the sensing zones. The application of EC techniques has proven to be highly effective in the determination of a diverse array of hormones including limited to testosterone, progesterone, estradiol, adrenaline, melatonin, dopamine and insulin [49,50]. In this section, three main types of MIP-based EC bio(sensors) developed for hormone detection are discussed: amperometric, voltammetric and amperometric techniques.

Voltammetry is a widely used EC method that is known for its high sensitivity and accuracy. This technique relies on the measurement of voltage current to detect and quantify the electroactive species present in a solution. The voltammetric technique offers a range of potentials for the working electrode to induce the oxidation or reduction of the analyte and generate a corresponding current response, which allows for the detection and quantification of the analyte. In recent years, voltammetric techniques such as linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), square wave voltammetry (SWV), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) have emerged as popular and widely used methods for analyzing EC reactions. These techniques have several advantages including ease of operation, the ability to provide accurate measurements, and high-speed identification of target molecules [51]. One of the key advantages of the sensing approaches based on voltammetry is their ability to provide highly sensitive measurements with minimal noise. In the development of EC bio(sensors), the electron transfer between the analyte and the transducer is typically mediated by a sensing element [52].

Due to the absence of physical contact between the analyte and the transducer, probes play critical role in the detection and quantification of the electroactive species in different samples [53]. Molecularly imprinted polymers have emerged as well-suited materials for use in voltammetric bio(sensors) in terms of hormones detection [54]. For instance, Manickam and colleagues [55], presented a novel method for the prompt determination of salivary cortisol, without the requirement of specific enzymes or antibodies. In this investigation, the application of MIPs was demonstrated by exploiting cortisol as a model analyte, and alongside that, EC overoxidation of polypyrrole was exploited to generate complementary imprinted sites and eliminate the entrapped template. The synthesis of a cortisol-imprinted polymer film was achieved through the electropolymerization of pyrrole over the electrode surface, in the presence of cortisol. The CV technique was employed in the described MIP-based sensor for the quantification of cortisol concentrations. The developed sensing platform demonstrated a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 pM, within a linear range from 1 pM to 10 μM. The prepared approach was used for the detection of cortisol in saliva samples of farm workers and was able to provide acceptable analytical results. In another recent publication, Liu et al. [56], introduced the synthesis of conductive MIP based on aniline-co-metanilic acid for application in the structure of EC sensor. The author reported a method for the self-assembly of a polymeric film imprinted with testosterone hormone molecules onto an electrode surface. The performance of the proposed sensor for testosterone detection was evaluated using CV test which showed a remarkable picomolar detection level. In 2020, Chacón and coworkers [57], designed a voltammetric sensor using MIPs for identification of the histamine hormone in wine. For this purpose, polymer beads were synthesized by utilizing the standard precipitation polymerization method. Subsequently, the beads were immobilized onto the graphite-epoxy composite (GEC) electrode surface through the sol-gel process. The developed platform was evaluated by the DPV technique providing a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 2.71–32.4 μM and acceptable sensitivity with a LOD of 1 μM. Recently, Zidarič and colleagues [58], constructed a MIP-based sensor for insulin hormone detection. In this work, synthetic MIP receptors were fabricated onto a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) via electropolymerization of pyrrole in the attendance of insulin hormone as the protein template. In order to investigate the conjugation of insulin to the imprinted cavities on the surface of modification electrode, the difference of voltage with/without insulin was measured by SWV test. The results demonstrated a linear response within the concentration range of 20–70 pM, additionally, the LOD was 1.9 pM which indicated a high sensitivity of the MIP receptors towards insulin detection. Furthermore, the MIP-SPCE sensor demonstrated its efficacy in the detection of insulin within a pharmaceutical sample. The sensor exhibited commendable accuracy, as indicated by an average recovery rate of 108.46 %. Additionally, it showcased impressive precision, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 7.23 %. In 2017, Cheng-Jung and co-workers [59], reported the development of an EC assay for the identification of insulin in serum samples. In this related study, the C-terminal polypeptide of insulin was utilized as the template molecule for the synthesis of a MIP layer on the surface of a sensing zone. The template molecule was self-assembled directly onto the electrode surface, followed by electrosynthesis of the MIP layer by using o-phenylenediamine as the functional monomer. While the exact mechanism by which the C-terminal polypeptide of insulin adsorbs onto the surface of the gold electrode was not fully elucidated by the authors, the effectiveness of this step was indirectly confirmed by monitoring the signal change of a redox probe. To promote optimal conditions for the re-adsorption of insulin onto the surface of the MIP sensor, the sensor was immersed into insulin standard solutions. Subsequently, the EC signal corresponding to various level of insulin were measured and a calibration curve was created based on the obtained data. The EC response of the MIP-based sensor towards insulin detection showed a linear relationship between the response current and insulin concentration in the range of 10 fM to 0.5 pM with a LOD of 7.24 fM. Similarly, Singh et al. [60], constructed a MIP-based sensor for thyroxine hormone detection. The successful fabrication of the analytical approach for the detection of thyroxine was accomplished by templating thyroxine into a polyaniline matrix supported on indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated glass electrodes. Polyaniline was synthesized via oxidative polymerization, and the process was optimized by utilizing Response Surface Methodology (RSM). By using CV, the analytical response of the reported MIP-based sensor was studied in different concentrations of thyroxine hormone, and this, in turn, provided it a LOD of 7.96 pM in the linear range of 5–50 pg mL−1. The designed electroanalytical assay exhibited a high rating of selectivity against different interferents, and alongside that, indicated significant potential for practical point-of-care applications.

To improve the performance of EC-based sensors, the MIP was combined with conducting polymers [61]. This synergistic concept allows for the development of bio(sensors) which exhibit high specificity and sensitivity towards hormone detection and considering this matter is critical for reliable and accurate sensing approaches in complex biological matrices [62]. For example, in 2017, Teng et al. [63], developed an EC sensor based on MIP for dopamine determination. In this study, three-dimensional structures were fabricated by electropolymerization of polypyrrole nanowires (PPyNWs) and MIP on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The modification GCE with PPyNWs and MIP exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity and large surface area towards the oxidation of dopamine. The electropolymerized MIP on the GCE surface revealed a large number of accessible surface imprints, which contributed to the significant enhancement in selectivity. Under normal circumstances, the calibration curve for dopamine detection was linear within the concentration range of 50 nM–100 μM. The outcomes of the actual sample determination indicate that the sensor is suitable for the analysis of dopamine in pharmaceutical samples. The use of PPyNWs with excellent properties such as high conductivity, ease of synthesis, and good catalytic capacity along with MIP led to the introduction of a robust analytical method. Polyacrylamide (PAM) is another applicable and biodegradable polymer that demonstrates several advantages including film-forming ability, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, chemical and mechanical resistance, and high hydrophilicity, all of which can be used in sensors based on MIP for hormones quantification [64]. The use of metal nanoparticles along with PAM and MIP can be very effective in increasing the overall sensitivity of the designed sensor. In this regard, Zhang et al. [65], developed a novel approach for the synthesis of a nickel (Ni)-PAM-MIP matrix on a sensing zone in favor of dopamine detection. This was achieved through a one-step electropolymerization process of acrylamide (AM) monomers in the presence of Ni and template molecules. Incorporation of Ni2+ ions served as both oxidants to facilitate the polymerization of AM monomers and bulking factors to construct a three-dimensional porous PAM-MIP matrix. The designed MIP-based voltammetric sensor performance was assessed in environments with pH values ranging from 5 to 9. The analytical performance of the designed sensor by DPV technique was investigated and it was revealed that there was a linear relationship in the linear range of 0.6–200 μM. Furthermore, the developed EC biosensor displayed remarkable reliability in detecting dopamine in human serum due to its outstanding anti-interference ability and long-term stability.

The integration of different nanomaterials and MIPs as probes improves their sensitivity and selectivity for hormone detection. Nanomaterials facilitate the transfer of charge on the EC electrode and increase the surface area [66]. Elaborately, transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) are a class of two-dimensional materials that have garnered significant attention in the field of materials science due to their unique electronic and optical properties [67]. Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) monolayers, including notable examples including tungsten disulfide (WS2) [68], molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) [69], molybdenum ditelluride (MoTe2) [70], tungsten diselenide (WSe2) [71], and molybdenum diselenide (MoSe2) [72], are characterized by their direct band gaps, which make them promising candidates for a wide range of applications in sensing. In 2019, the Lee group [73], reported their findings regarding the performance of a 17β-estradiol (E2) sensor based on the functionalized MIP with WS2. In detail, the coating was formed by using electropolymerization of aniline and a metanilic acid conductive polymer onto a screen-printed electrodes (SPE) substrate. To evaluate the practical application of the developed MIP-SPE sensor, eel serum samples with varying concentrations of E2 in the range of 28.2 ± 3.6 to 73.0 ± 11.6 pg mL−1 were analyzed. Gold nanoparticles, as another type of metal nanoparticles, gained significant attention in the field of hormones sensing due to their unique properties. One of the key advantages of AuNPs is their ability to functionalize with various polymers such as MIPs and biomolecules for the development of highly sensitive and selective sensors for hormones determination [74]. Laza et al. [75], designed an efficient and robust EC sensor using a MIP for the quantitative measurement of progesterone hormone. For this purpose, AuNPs were deposited on the surface of the EC electrode by exploiting the EC technique. Following the EC deposition of AuNPs onto GCE, the next step was to deposit the MIP onto the surface. This was achieved through the electropolymerization of aminophenol in the presence of the target analyte (Fig. 1 A). The developed sensor exhibited favorable analytical performance for the detection of progesterone, with a linear response ranging from 0.2 to 125 nM and a LOD of 0.17 nM. In addition, this sensor was able to successfully detect the target hormone in calf serum samples. Similarly, another novel voltammetric analytical approach based on MIP for histamine detection was constructed by Li and co-workers [76]. In this study, the decoration of GCE with AuNPs and L-cysteine could provide an appropriate substrate for the immobilization of a MIP layer. In detail, the decoration of AuNP-modified GCE with L-cysteine film via an Au–S covalent bond could present a suitable immobilization surface of the MIP layer and ensure its stable connection to the electrode surface through hydrogen bonding. To evaluate the performance of the developed assay, the DPV technique was operated according to the sample containing various concentrations of histamine. Elaborately, a direct relationship was observed between the concentration of histamine and the EC signal. The designed platform could successfully detect the target in the linear range of 1–107 μM and a LOD of 0.6 μM. Moreover, the reported sensor demonstrated high efficacy in terms of the detection of histamine concentration in alcoholic beverages, specifically beer and wine. The recoveries achieved were within the range of 89.6–100.2 % (with RSDs ranging from 0.4 to 5.4 %) for beer, and 87.4–103.9 % (with RSDs ranging from 0.1 to 0.8 %) for wine. In another example, Huang and colleagues [77], designed a molecularly imprinted-based EC aptasensor for sensitive progesterone detection using tin oxide (SnO2)-graphene (SnO2-Gr) nanomaterial and AuNPs (Fig. 1B). The development of this composite material with the combination of SnO2 and graphene has shown a significant improvement in progesterone absorption capacity owing to its large specific surface area and excellent conductivity. A biocompatible monomer, aptamer, was immobilized onto a modified electrode surface through the formation of Au–S bonds with AuNPs. An electropolymerized MIP layer was developed using progesterone as the template molecule and p-aminothiophenol as the chemical functional monomer. The developed molecularly imprinted EC aptasensor exhibited excellent selectivity towards progesterone owing to the synergistic effect of combining MIP and aptamer as dual recognition elements. Under optimal circumstances, the prepared aptassay could able to detect progesterone in the presence of the EC probe([Fe(CN)6]3-/4) and this, in turn, demonstrated that the current of signal was decreased with the increase in the concentration of the target molecule. Notably, the platform had a LOD of 1.73 fM in the linear range of 10 fM to 10 μM. The satisfactory recovery achieved in both milk and tap water samples demonstrated the high potential of the developed sensor for use in food and environmental analysis. Another important metallic-based nanomaterial is metallic Ni which enjoys several advantages such as easy preparation and well-controlled size. To illustrate, hollow Ni nanospheres (hNiNS), which emerged from hollow architecture and nanoplates, can be an attractive option for use in the structure of MIP-based sensors for hormones quantification [78]. In this regard, Liu et al. [79], designed an EC sensor using MIP-hNiNS nanocomposite for sensitive identification of dopamine hormone. In this work, the hNiNS support nanomaterial and MIP layer could increase conductivity and improve the selectivity of the sensor for dopamine hormone, respectively. In the developed EC, the conjugation of the modification surface with the target led to a linear relationship between peak anodic current and dopamine concentration in the range of 50 fM to 50 pM, and a LOD of 17 fM was obtained in this range. Nanocomposites consisting of MIPs and conductive metallic nanomaterials such as silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have been used to enhance the EC signal in assays in favor of hormones determination due to their ability to increase the binding kinetics, mass transfer rate and binding capacity [80]. Recently, another EC sensor based on molecularly imprinted poly(p-aminophenol) supported by AgNPs capped with 2-mercaptobenzoxazole for E2 detection was developed by Regasa and Nyokong [81]. The proposed MIP composite-based assay was prepared through the electropolymerization of a functional monomer in the presence of E2 as a template. Under optimal conditions, the measurement of EC signal in the prepared substrate (MIP-AgNP/GCE) revealed a linear calibration curve in the range of 10 pM–100 nM.

The high catalytic activity of platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs), as another member of metallic-based nanomaterials family, can improve the mass transfer rate and binding kinetics of MIPs, resulting in increased selectivity and sensitivity towards the hormones detection. In addition, the integration of PtNPs in the structure of MIP-based sensors can increase the stability and durability of the sensing platforms [84]. For instance, Koçak and Akyüz [85], proposed a novel dopamine EC assay that utilized a MIP on a carbon electrode for efficient detection of dopamine. The sensing platform was constructed by forming a thin film of thiophene acetic acid-dopamine on the surface of the electrode via electropolymerization. Indeed, the modified electrode surface was decorated with PtNPs, which were deposited onto the electrode to enhance the catalytic activity of the sensor. To remove dopamine, which was used as a template molecule during the formation of the MIP layer, elution was performed using suitable chemicals. In this work, the comparison of calibration curves of different concentrations was performed to identify the target hormone. The performance of the proposed sensor was measured by using the DPV test which revealed a direct relationship between the current intensity and the concentration of dopamine. Another metallic nanomaterial, with suitable electrocatalytic properties, has been used to improve MIP-based sensing approaches properties for identifying hormones is palladium nanoparticles (PdNPs) [86]. For example, Chen and colleagues [82], reported the synthesis of a novel nanocomposite consisting of MIP-coated PdNPs, through the sol-gel method, on the surface of the EC electrode for norepinephrine determination (Fig. 1C). Elaborately, the incorporation of PdNPs and silica-based MIP led to favorable electrocatalytic and chemical properties. The voltammetric technique was employed to measure of conjugation the target with modification EC electrode with the linear range of 0.5–80 μM. In addition, the designed sensing platform was an appropriate diagnostic tool for selective, sensitive, and rapid identification of noradrenaline in urine and injection samples. In another similar example, Cherian et al. [83], developed an EC method using a composite film for progesterone detection (Fig. 1D). For this purpose, PdNPs and 3-thiophene acetic acid (3-TAA) were integrated by molecular imprinting technology to generate a composite film. In this investigation, progesterone molecule was utilized as a template to generate molecular imprints via electropolymerization on the surface of a Carbon Fibre Paper (CFP) electrode. Under optimal conditions, different concentration of the target was investigated on the surface of prepared sensor (MIP/PdNPs/CFP) by DPV test which revealed that the oxidation current increased with the enhancement in progesterone hormone concentration from 0.1 to 110 nM. In the example, the incorporation of PdNPs in the composite film provided several benefits for the EC oxidation of progesterone. Firstly, the conductivity enhancement of the film by PdNPs facilitated the EC reaction. Additionally, the high surface area of the PdNPs allowed for a larger number of binding sites for the target molecule, leading to improved selectivity and sensitivity.

Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) are a kind of nanomaterials which possess magnetic properties [87]. One of the most notable characteristics of these nanomaterials is their responsiveness to an external magnetic field, which allows for their facile manipulation and separation [88]. owing to their specific thermal, magnetic, optical, electrical, and chemical properties, they are of great interest in diagnostic tools such as sensors [89]. Magnetic MIPs (MagMIPs) have been developed by incorporating MNPs into the MIP matrix. In the realm of EC detection of hormones, the application of MagMIPs has proven advantageous due to the enhanced accessibility of the hormones to the selective cavities of the imprinted polymer, as well as the effective immobilization of the hormones on the electrode surface [90]. In an EC study, Silva and Pereira [91], designed an electrode based on carbon paste electrode modified with MagMIPs (CPE-MagMIP) for the quantitative analysis of E2. The performance of the proposed sensor was evaluated by an analytical curve demonstrating a linear response range from 0.5 to 14.0 μM. Beyond the concentration range of 14.0 μM, the analytical signal obtained from the sensor exhibited a non-linear increase that the process of adsorption of E2 on the decoration electrode surface has reached a state of saturation. The LOD of the proposed sensor was determined to be 0.13 μM. In addition, the designed sensor under optimal conditions was able to successfully detect E2 in water samples (R2 = 0.998). In another example, Silva et al. [92], fabricated an efficient EC sensor exploiting MagMIPs, as a probe, for the identification of E2. In terms of construction probe, MagMIPs were synthesized by using iron oxide (Fe3O4)@silicon dioxide (SiO2) core@shell nanoparticles coated with a methacrylamide polymer matrix cross-linked with ethylene glycol dimethacrylate. In this study, the modification of EC electrode by MagMIPs could bring about selectively absorbed substrate of E2. To elaborate, the presence of MNPs improved the immobilization of MIPs on the surface of a carbon paste-based electrode. The measurements were conducted by using the differential pulse adsorptive stripping voltammetry technique, which is sensitive detection of the target with a LOD of 0.02 μM.

In recent years, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have garnered significant attention as promising materials for modifying electrodes [93]. This is due to the unique properties of MOFs including their accessible cage structure, appropriate pore volume, large surface area, ordered arrangement, and high biocompatibility [94]. By combining MOFs with auxiliary materials, researchers have designed novel probes which can be used in the structure of sensors for the sensitive detection of hormones [95]. Among different MOFs, ZIF-67 is a widely employed and highly favorable MOF for use as EC probes [96]. For example, Zhang and co-workers [97], developed a hybrid material of ZIF-67/Nafion and dopamine molecular imprinting via a hydrothermal method. The synthesized hybrid material was utilized to modify the surface of a GCE for the preparation of an EC assay which aimed to use as a sensing approach of dopamine. Under normal circumstances, the EC behavior of dopamine was investigated and demonstrated that the oxidation peak current was enhanced with increasing dopamine concentration. The developed voltammetric sensor exhibited a remarkable linear response within the ranges of 0.08–500 μM and a LOD of 0.0308 μM.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have emerged as a highly attractive class of nanomaterials in the field of science and technology [98]. The unique properties of carbon-based materials have made them highly prized owing to the existence of diverse allotropes of carbon including well-known phases such as amorphous carbon [99], graphite [100] and diamonds [101] as well as newly discovered materials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [102], fullerene [103], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [104], graphene oxide (GO) [105], reduced GO (rGO) [106], carbon black (CB) [107], and carbon nanofibers (CNFs) [108]. These nanomaterials possess exceptional mechanical, electrical, and optical properties, all of which can be exploited for the specific detection of hormones [109]. To illustrate, the high surface area and diverse functional groups in carbon-based nanomaterials introduce a brilliant substrate for binding to a wide range of molecules, especially MIPs [110]. In a recent study, Silva and Pereira [111], proposed a voltammetric sensor utilizing a combination of MIP and CB for the detection of E2 in river water samples. In detail, the modification of the electrode with the CB and MIP led to a significant increase in sensitivity. In this investigation, with the gradual increase in E2 concentration from 0.1 to 23 μM, the anode peak currents also changed linearly. Similarly, Nekoueian et al. [112], developed an ultrasensitive sensor based on integrating carbon-based hybrid nanomaterials with molecular imprinting technology for the detection of basal dopamine. Subsequently, CNFs were fabricated by using plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) on a tetrahedral amorphous carbon (ta-C) thin layer on silicon wafers. The obtained ta-C/CNFs were coated with dopamine-imprinted polypyrrole by using the EC technique, resulting in the formation of MIP. The developed three-dimensional MIP receptors demonstrated the high potential to accurately detect trace amounts of dopamine in various complex biological environments. Specifically, the MIP receptors were able to determine dopamine levels with high sensitivity and selectivity with LOD = 5.43 nM. In another example, Sun and colleagues [113], designed an efficient and simple voltammetric sensor exploiting graphene-assisted molecular imprinting technology for melatonin. In this work, the conductivity of the electrode was improved by incorporating 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT) and graphene. Furthermore, to enhance the binding of EDOT to the imprinted layer, 4-aminothiophenol (4-AMP) was used for functionalization. The 4-AMP acted as a linker molecule allowing for a strong and stable binding of EDOT to the imprinted layer. Electropolymerization was then performed to deposit the imprinted membrane. The designed MIPs/4-AMP/poly EDOT/GCE was investigated under optimal conditions with LSV and it was revealed that the current of the EC signal was decreased with increasing melatonin concentration in the range of 0–100 μM. In 2021, another minimized voltammetric approach based on the decoration of homemade micropipette tip carbon paste electrode (CPE) for dopamine quantification was developed by Wang and co-workers [114]. In this study, MIP membranes consisting polypyrrole and GO were synthesized and modified onto the surface of a CPE micropipette tip. The effectiveness of the proposed method was evaluated by using the DPV test. The results of this study showed that the oxidation peak current increased in proportion to the concentration of the template molecule. This approach was able to successfully detect dopamine hormone in the linear range of 64 nM to 0.2 mM and a LOD of 10 nM. In addition, the designed sensor to identify the target hormone was tested in the human urine sample and was able to display a good recovery rate in the range of 95.2 %–104 %. In another study, a conducting polymer film imprinted with dopamine was synthesized using 5-amino 8-hydroxy quinoline (AHQ) and electrodeposited onto a rGO-modified GCE [115]. The modified electrode with an MIP exhibited a high affinity towards dopamine, owing to the attendance of imprinted sites formed through hydrogen bonding interactions between dopamine and the poly AHQ membrane. The use of rGO in the structure of the probe increased the electrode surface and electron transfer kinetics. In another scientific investigation [116], a nanocomposite was fabricated for the detection of adrenaline by synthesizing MIPs via EC polymerization of poly(3-aminophenylboronic acid) (PAPBA) on multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs) (Fig. 2 A). The study results revealed that the PAPBA(MIPs)/MWCNTs sensor exhibited remarkable selectivity towards adrenaline, even in the presence of numerous potential interferents. The sensor optimal conditions were carefully established, and it demonstrated a wide linear range of 0.2–800 μM for detecting adrenaline, with high sensitivity, excellent discrimination and an impressively LOD of 35 nM. In addition to the commonly used carbon materials, three-dimensional graphene aerogels (GAs) have emerged as a high potential probe for hormones. These aerogels are fabricated through the interconnection of graphene sheets, resulting in a highly porous structure with a large surface area [117]. In this regard, Ma et al. [118], suggested the combination of MIP and three-dimensional GAs decorated with MWCNTs for the EC measurement of dopamine. The incorporation of conductive MWCNTs led to the significant enhancement of the electrical conductivity and EC performance of the composite aerogel. In addition, the using of MIP enhanced the selectivity of the detection platform. The analytical performance of the MIP assay for detecting dopamine was evaluated using the DPV technique under optimized conditions. By using the prepared sensor, different concentrations of the target hormone in the range of 5 nM–20 μM were investigated and the LOD of 1.67 nM was obtained. Nickel-based nanomaterials, as one of the important types of metal nanoclusters, have garnered significant attention as probes on the surface of EC electrodes owing to their favorable properties such as commendable electrocatalytic activity, high electrical conductivity, and cost-effectiveness. Furthermore, the application of CNTs can serve as an effective layer for anchoring Ni atoms, thereby enhancing the stability and dispersion of Ni nanoclusters. Furthermore, this concept can provide a large number of imprinting sites for the molecularly imprinted sensing platform [119]. In light of this, Duan and colleagues [120], carried out a study in which they exploited bamboo-like CNTs doped with nitrogen (N) and loaded with Ni nanoclusters, which were synthesized by using a one-pot pyrolysis method (Fig. 2B). In this sensing platform, the presence of cortisol lead to increased intensity of EC signal, which brought about a broad linear range and a LOD of 10 fM to 1 nM and 2.37 fM, respectively. Furthermore, the developed sensing platform was successfully utilized for the identification of cortisol in saliva samples.

Over the last few years, the combination of magnetic nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials, and MIP has led to the emergence of a new and sensitive probe, which is called hybrid probe, for identifying hormones. In a recent study by Li et al. [121], a novel molecularly imprinted-based EC platform has been developed for the detection of E2 in water. The sensor platform is based on Fe3O4 nanobeads immobilized on graphene and incorporates a MIP layer (Fe3O4-MIP@rGO). The MIP layer was synthesized by using a reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization technique, which enabled the precise control of the polymerization process and the synthesis of well-defined polymeric structures. The designed sensor could detect the hormone by changing the response current before and after binding with a LOD of 0.819 nM. Having advantages such as low toxicity, high conductivity, unique quantum confinement, acceptable electrocatalytic activity, and edge effects have made GQDs doped with N and sulfur (S) become an ideal electron mediator [122]. Nitrogen (N)-doped and sulfur-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs) have widespread used as EC probes in MIP-based assays. For example, Mahnashi et al. [123], proposed a voltammetric sensor based on MIP for the detection of histamine. In this scientific investigation, authors employed an electro-polymerization technique to deposit a functional monomer (pyrrole) onto two distinct materials, specifically AuNPs functionalized Fe-MOF (FeBDC) and N–S co-doped GQDs (N, S-GQDs). The response of the modified electrode with/without the target was measured by voltammetric technique. Elaborately, the EC signal on the MIP-Au@Fe-BDC/N, S-GQDs/GCE modified electrode increased proportionally with the concentration of histamine in the range of 0.078–250 nM. Furthermore, the sensor was used to quantify histamine in human serum and canned tuna samples. To illustrate, the recovery percentage and relative standard deviations percentage (RSD) of the modification electrode were determined to be in the range of 97.2–103 % and 1.8–3.8 %, respectively. The two-dimensional multilevel accordion MXene exhibits highly desirable properties including a large specific surface area, an open interlayer channel, and excellent metallic conductivity [124]. To elaborate, MXene, a newly discovered active EC probe, has received significant interest in various sensing applications, hormones detection included [125]. Thereby, MXene-modified MIP can be considered one of the solutions to provide more stable MIP-based probes. Importantly, MXene lamellae are prone to restacking, which can hinder their intrinsic EC properties. To overcome the limitation of restacking in MXene, carbon materials such as carbon nanohorns (CNHs), have been employed as intercalation agents between MXene flakes [126]. In 2021, Chen et al. [127], attempted to use MXene/CNHs/MIPs-based probe to provide a high potential EC probe for the quantitative analysis of adrenaline. For this purpose, a new catalytic substrate consisting of MXene and CNH was introduced in the form of a composite material (MXene/CNH). Following the development of the MXene/CNH composite, a MIP film was fabricated utilizing a mixed solution containing adrenaline (the template molecule) and hydroxymethyl (CH2OH)-EDOT (a functional monomer) through an electro-polymerization process. In this work, the optimized conditions were utilized to perform a DPV test for the detection of adrenaline on the poly EDOT–CH2OH–MIP/MXene/CNHs/GCE. In detail, there is a linear relationship between the concentration of adrenaline and the EC signal.

According to the comparison of different reported EC bio(sensor), the high importance of using nanomaterials along with MIPs is quite clear. Metal nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials, and hybrid nanomaterials have been widely used to develop MIP-based voltammetry sensors. However, there is a shortage in the integration of MIPs with silica-based nanomaterials for hormones quantification. To illustrate, different format of silica nanomaterials with unique chemical and physical properties can introduce sensitive and selective EC-based analytical approaches.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) has attracted considerable attention in recent years due to its numerous advantages including fast analysis, high miniaturization ability, sensitivity, and reproducibility for both quantitative and qualitative analysis of interfacial properties related MIPs [128,129]. Amperometry, as another reliable and well-established EC technique, involves the measurement and control of small currents by using a working electrode at a constant potential. In addition, the amperometric technique employs a potent step signal, which is applied between the reference electrode and the working electrode immersed in a solution containing an electroactive sample. Amperometric assays function on the principle of recording the current as a function of time, through the application of a constant potential [130].

Among different evaluation factors of EC bio(sensors) based on MIP for hormone detection, portability can be considered one of the important features for improving the performance of sensing approaches [131]. Generally, although researchers attempted to improve the sensitivity and selectivity of EC biosensors by integrating various nanomaterials and bioreceptors in the structure of these biosensors, conventional developed platforms suffer from a lack of portability. For example, Liu et al. [132], designed a MIP-based sensor using 3, 6-diamino-9-ethylcarbazole monomer for 17-β-estradiol determination in biological fluids (Fig. 3 ). The prepared assay operated according to the detection changes in interfacial impedance resulting from the conjugation of the 17-β-estradiol molecule by the MIP film. In other words, the conjugation on the surface of the sensing zone led to an increase in charge-transfer resistance value. This phenomenon can be detected by employing the EIS technique, which enables the measurement of interfacial impedance changes between the MIP layer and the analyte solution with a linear range of 1 aM–10 μM. In addition, the LOD obtained for the reported sensor was equal to 0.36 aM. In 2016, another impedimetric sensor for the ultrasensitive detection of testosterone hormone exploiting MIP-GO sheet was reported by a similar group [133]. The remarkable enhancement in sensitivity of the MIP sensor platform can be attributed to the unique nanosheet structure and high surface area of GO. Under normal conditions, the developed sensor platform exhibited an impressive linear range spanning from 1 fM to 1 μM and a LOD of 0.4 fM. Some of scholars pay attention to bioreceptors on the surface of nanomaterials for providing more efficient sensing platforms. For instance, Mahmoud et al. [134], fabricated a novel aptasensor based on AuNPs/for the detection of histamine in canned tuna and human plasma. For this purpose, the decoration of carboxylated CNTs with AuNPs (AuNPs/CNTs) brought about excellent substrate for immobilization of aptamer on the surface of GCE. The resulting AuNPs/CNTs/GCE platform exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards histamine. The performance of the MIP-aptamer/AuNPs/CNTs/GCE platform in detecting histamine was evaluated via the EIS test. The platform was exposed to histamine, which resulted in a concentration-dependent decrease in the current of the redox probe.

These previous examples of reported MIP-based impedance biosensors lack of ability of point of care analysis. Indium tin oxide (ITO) electrodes, as one of the miniaturized sensing zones, can be considered as a point of care sensor due to their portability nature [135]. In 2022, Pareek and co-workers [136], reported a high performance of EC-based analytical approaches for the detection of follicle-stimulating hormone in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). The highly selective and specific recognition of follicle-stimulating hormone by exploiting a MIP film which was achieved through electropolymerization onto a Ni cobaltite (NiCo2O4)/rGO-modified ITO electrode. When the target was added to the system, the performance of prepared sensor was evaluated by the EIS technique which demonstrated a linear range and LOD of 0.1 pM–1 μM and 0.1 pM, respectively. Another example of a portable sensing platform based on MIPs is the application of smartphones, which provides excellent reader and analyzer. To elaborate, smartphones enjoy powerful cameras, ample storage memory, high processing power and adjustable screens, all of which can enhance point-of-care hormone detection [137]. For instance, Venkatesh and colleagues [138], used a MIP layer on the sensing zone of a flexible substrate for introducing an impedance sensor in terms of histamine identification. To demonstrate the feasibility of the approach, a semi-quantitative histamine sensing device was fabricated based on the smartphone communicated to the Bluetooth module. To illustrate, the reading and analyzing EC signal, which is generated on the surface of the electrode and sent by Bluetooth, was conducted by a custom build app on the smartphone. The device was able to provide accurate and reliable testing results for histamine in various test solutions, as well as in real seafood samples. The designed sensor is capable of detecting histamine within the range of 100–500 ppm. Wearable microfluidic devices have emerged as a novel and promising technique that enables personalized monitoring of physiological states, and alongside that, they offer unique insights into various aspects of human health [139]. These devices incorporating sensing units, microfluidic chips, transmission electronics, and signal processing, can be seamlessly attached to the human skin [140]. This feature renders them highly suitable for non-invasive, hassle-free, and real-time analysis of biomarkers present in sweat [139]. In this regard, Mei et al. [141], developed a flexible EC assay using MIP based on the microfluidic system for the quantitative analysis of cortisol in sweat (Fig. 4 ). The sensor was designed by using polyethylene terephthalate (PET) as the substrate, comprising two distinct layers. The lower layer was composed of an electrode that was modified with MIPs for sensing purposes. The upper layer, on the other hand, was a microfluidic layer made of nanofibers. This layer facilitated the spontaneous pumping of sweat for analysis by the underlying sensing layer. In order to improve both the stability and sensitivity of the sensor, a three-dimensional CNF-AuNPs with a porous and interconnected structure was employed as a matrix for the deposition of MIP. Additionally, the incorporation of Prussian blue nanoparticles (PBNPs) as a "built-in" redox probe within the MIP structure provided in situ sensing of sweat substances. Under optimal condition, the different concentration of the target hormone was investigated by chronoamperometry technique which revealed that an increase in cortisol hormone concentration decreased in the response current. The reported sensing device was able to detect cortisol with a linear relationship from 1 nM to 1 μM and a LOD of 0.35 nM.

The scarcity of impedance and amperometric sensors based on MIPs for detection of hormones have caused limitations for compressive review of them, however, the comparison of the developed analytical approaches demonstrates that these techniques can play an important role in portability. The need for portable sensors has become increasingly crucial in contemporary research and should be given due consideration by researchers. The development of portable sensors has opened up a range of possibilities for on-site, real-time analysis, and has the potential to transform hormones detection.

According to the advantages of EC sensors such as identification of very low concentrations of target analytes, fast response time in analyte quantitative analysis and cost-effectiveness, they can be considered as suitable analytical approaches for identifying hormones. However, EC sensors often require regular calibration to maintain accuracy. In addition, the interpretation of the data and stability can suppose other challenges in terms of developing these sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical bio(sensors) based on MIPs for hormones detection
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, optical sensors have emerged as a highly valuable analytical tool operating based on the monitoring of intensity changes in light beams or modulations in their phases during interactions with physical systems. Optical sensors have demonstrated significant advantages over traditional sensors, offering increased sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, speed of analysis, and specificity. Sensing technology based on optical techniques has become a vital platform for various applications including biomedical imaging [142], chemical sensing [143] and environmental monitoring [144]. Optical platforms have been categorized into five distinct groups, each offering practical and useful applications in contemporary research. These groups including surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), surface plasmon resonance (SPR), UV/vis, colorimetric sensing, fluorescence sensing and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [145]. This section aims to explore and discuss the application of several optical methods based on MIPs for the detection of hormones.

One commonly employed and practical optical analytical technique is the fluorimeter [146]. This instrument utilizes ultraviolet light to excite the electrons of various specimens such as different dyes, nanoparticles, and oligonucleotides. As a result of the excitation process, the specimens under investigation emit fluorescent light, which can be accurately measured and analyzed. These measurements can provide valuable insights into the physical and chemical properties of the specimens [147]. The features of high sensitivity and selectivity, as well as their ability to save time and reduce costs, have made MIP-based fluorescence sensors a popular choice for hormone detection.

In the context of MIPs, functional monomers refer to monomers that possess a reactive or functional group capable of interacting with the template molecule during polymerization [148]. The selection of a functional monomer is a crucial determinant of the selectivity and sensitivity of MIP-based sensors due to it affects the binding affinity and specificity of the MIP [149]. Various functional monomers, such as methacrylic acid, itaconic acid, 5- indolyl boronic acid, citric acid, N- methacryloyl-L-tryptophan and N- methacryloyl-L-aspartic acid, have been used in the synthesis of MIPs for various hormones quantification. In 2017, Wang et al. [150], reported on a novel approach utilizing surface molecular imprinting on quantum dots (QDs) modified by ionic liquid (IL) for the detection of histamine. In this study, a sensing material for the detection of the target hormone was synthesized using a facile one-pot approach on the surface of cadmium selenide (CdSe)/zinc sulfide (ZnS) QDs. This process involved the introduction of an IL to the QDs via electrostatic interactions, resulting in the formation of a layer of MIP on the QD surface. In addition, it should be noted that the functional monomer used to prepare MIP was methacrylic acid. The fluorescence intensity of the prepared probe gradually increased with the increase in the concentration of histamine hormone. To evaluate the analytical performance of the QDs@IL@MIP sensor, a LOD and linear range were determined by fluorescence test which revealed that the QDs@IL@MIP probe demonstrated a linear range of 0.449–2.249 mM for histamine. In another similar example, Ming and co-workers [151], also used methacrylic acid as a functional monomer to prepare MIP. In this work, a novel composite material consisting of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and MIPs was synthesized by using a surface imprinting technique combined with precipitation polymerization (Fig. 5 ). The resulting material, referred to as MagMIPs, demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity for the fluorescent detection of E2. The detection mechanism was based on the competitive desorption of fluorescein, a fluorescent indicator, from the MagMIPs. The regulation of fluorescein release and subsequent enhancement of fluorescence signal can be attributed to the competitive rebinding of E2 to its corresponding recognition sites. A direct correlation has been observed between the concentration of added E2 and the resulting increase in fluorescence intensity within the solution. Within the range of 0.10–70 μM, a linear relationship has been observed between the concentration of E2 and the corresponding increase in fluorescence intensity. In addition, the LOD of 0.03 μM in this concentration range was obtained by the prepared probe. In spiked lake and river water samples, the recovery of E2 was in the range of 98.2 %–103.8 % and RSD between 1.1 % and 3.8 %. In a recent study, Murase and colleagues [152], synthesized core-shell-type MIP particles (MIP-NPs) for developing a fluorescence polarization-based sensing nano-platform for the competitive binding assay of cortisol hormone. For this purpose, itaconic acid was employed as an additional functional monomer with styrene serving as a comonomer and divinylbenzene as the crosslinker. The assay employed dansyl-cortisol as the sensing molecule and achieved a remarkable level of sensitivity, with an LOD of approximately 80 nM. In 2017, Zhou and coworkers [153], conducted a study on the detection of the dopamine hormone using MIP/GQDs (MIP/GQDs) and poly(indolylboronic acid) (PIn-BAc). During the preparation of the MIPs@PIn-BAc/GQDs system, the introduction of dopamine resulted in the fluorescence quenching of the nanocomposites and agglomeration of the particles. These phenomena were attributed to the covalent interaction between the catechol group present in dopamine and the boronic acid. The experimental results obtained from the study showed that the MIPs@PIn-BAc/GQDs system is capable of detecting a broad range of dopamine concentrations, with a linear response observed over a range of 5 nM to 1.2 μM. Additionally, the system demonstrated a low LOD of 2.5 nM indicating its high sensitivity towards dopamine detection. In another study conducted by Zhou et al. [154], a probe for dopamine hormone sensing was developed based on polyindole/GQDs MIPs (PIn/GQDs@MIPs). The functional monomer used here to prepare MIP was citric acid. Under normal circumstances, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the PIn/GQDs@MIPs sensor increased in a linear relationship with the concentrations of dopamine in the range of 0.5–1200 nM. Similarly, Bhogal and co-workers [155], reported a new method for the selective and sensitive fluorescent detection of E2. The approach involved the self-polymerization of dopamine, by a functional monomer, on the surface fluorescent CDs, resulting in the formation of polydopamine-coated molecularly imprinted CDs (CDs@MI-PDA). The developed sensor utilized a photo-induced electron transfer mechanism to quench the fluorescence intensity of the conjugation E2 with fluorescent CDs. The results of this platform showed a LOD of 0.34 ng mL−1 and linearity in the concentration range from 1 to 50 ng mL−1. In addition, this platform could be applied to milk and water samples (river and tap water) with a recovery of 96.4–102.2 %.

Recently, the substitute of novel fluorescence probes such as functionalized lanthanide with conventional probes such as QDs and CDs attracted much attention in favor of hormones determination. To elaborate, lanthanide suffers from weak fluorescence characteristics which can be addressed by incorporating metal chelated complexes. Thereby, brilliant properties of lanthanide complex including light stability, long lifetime, negligible autofluorescence, sharp absorption and emission bands, lack of photobleaching and photoblinking introduce a novel and efficient fluorescence probe in hormones sensing [156]. For instance, Özgür and colleagues [157], exploited a lanthanide metal terbium(III) to develop fluorescent MIPs-based sensor in terms of quantitative determination of melatonin. In this study, two amino acid-based functional monomers including N-methacryloyl-L-aspartic acid and N-methacryloyl L-tryptophan were employed to coordinate melatonin and terbium(III) ions, respectively. Furthermore, the polymerizability of lanthanide complexes facilitated their incorporation into the polymeric chain during the synthesis of the fluorescent MIP nanoparticles. To elaborate, this concept allowed for the homogeneous distribution of the lanthanide complexes throughout the polymer matrix, resulting in closer orientation to the imprinted cavities for selective melatonin recognition. The study employed two different high-affinity orientations to attach melatonin to the melatonin-imprinted fluorescent nanoparticles. The performance of the prepared probe was evaluated by analyzing its linearity in detecting melatonin concentrations.

Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) emerged as a powerful analytical method which can be used in diverse fields such as surface science, food safety and detection of biomarkers [158]. The popularity of SERS can be attributed to its label-free nature, easy execution, and high specificity and sensitivity. The sensing approach based on SERS operates by measuring the Raman scattering signal of analytes in close proximity to a rough metallic surface, typically composed of materials such as silver or gold [159]. This section will focus on the development of MIP-based SERS sensors for the identification of hormones. Elaborately, the integration of SERS sensors and MIPs represents a highly promising approach for the development of sensors with enhanced selectivity and sensitivity toward hormones detection. Silica-based nanomaterials and metallic-based nanomaterials can act as SERS active sites for hormone detection. To illustrate, the integration of porous silica and AgNPs substrates with molecular imprinting has been demonstrated to provide significant advantages in terms of sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of hormones [160]. In this regard, Mugo et al. [161], presented a novel approach for the selective determination of E2 using SERS. The approach was based on a phytoplankton-derived nanoporous biogenic silica (BS) scaffold, which was modified with a polymethacrylate E2 MIP film, resulting in the MIP@BS platform. The utilization of BS nanoparticles as support for MIPs and as an active substrate provided high surface area adhesion and signal enhancement agents, respectively. The analytical performance of the MIP@BS SERS sensors was assessed for the detection of E2 through the treatment of 0.1–4 ng mL−1 E2 standards. The examination of the obtained results for this investigation revealed that the response increased proportionally with the target concentration. The designed sensor showed a LOD of 0.073 ng mL−1, which is a suitable analytical response. The efficacy of the MIP@BS SERS assay was further demonstrated through their successful application for the determination of E2 in milk. In another example, Fan and co-workers [162], used AgNPs to prepare a SERS sensor. In this study, a novel molecularly imprinted monolithic column embedded with AgNPs (MIMC@Ag) was synthesized by using an in-situ polymerization technique. The process involved the use of a template and porogen inside a capillary tube, followed by the reduction of a Ag precursor and subsequent removal of the template and porogen to enable the detection of cortisol through Raman spectroscopy. This platform had a good linear relationship in the target hormone concentration range from 0.1 μM to 1 mM with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.978. In addition, it should be noted that this approach had a LOD of 0.1 μM.

The combination of a semiconductor substrate with a noble metal has led to enhanced Raman intensities through a combination of two mechanisms including plasmon resonances and charge-transfer processes [163]. Recently, Chen and colleagues [164], reported the successful development of a molecularly imprinted SERS sensor with high sensitivity for the selective detection of histamine. In this work, a combination of AgNPs and two semiconductors was employed as the SERS substrate. Elaborately, the SERS effect was provided by the combination of plasmon resonances of the AgNPs and charge-transfer processes occurring between the semiconductors and the AgNPs. In the presence of the target, the relationship between Raman intensity and the logarithm of histamine hormone concentration was investigated in a range of 10 nM to 1 mM. In another example, Zhang et al. [165], introduced a novel approach for the synthesis of a core-shell structured polydopamine molecularly imprinted AuNPs (AuNP@MIP-PDA), which was subsequently incorporated into Fe MOF materials to create a well-defined hexagonal microspindle structure of AuNP@MIP-PDA@MIL-101(Fe). The integration of SERS activity of AuNPs, the specific detection sites provided by MIPs, and enrichment capacity of MIL-101 brought about new emerging nanohybrids that could be considered as highly selective SERS sensors for detection of E2 in milk. In this approach, with the increase in the target analyte concentration, the intensity of the SERS band also increased, which indicates the existence of a linear relationship between the concentration and the intensity of the SERS band. The prepared probe had a LOD of 0.195 fM in the linear range of 10 fM to 1 μM.

It should be noted that few MIP-based SERS sensors have been developed for the detection of hormones. Considering that these platforms have good diagnostic ability, it is expected that they will receive more attention.

Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) is an optical technique that is widely employed for the measurement of changes in the refractive index of a gold surface that result from the attachment of molecules to the probe. This sensing platform could able to label-free detection of any molecular interaction at the surface of the probe [166]. The technique of molecular imprinting has played a crucial role in the development of effective sensing assays. In this part of the review, we will examine MIP-based SPR sensors that have been developed for the detection of hormones.

The preparation of MIPs is a critical step that requires careful consideration of factors that can impact their performance as sensing platforms. In this regard, simplicity and ease of synthesis are considered to be influential factors that can significantly impact the overall efficiency and practicality of MIPs [167]. The comparison of single-layer MIPs and double-layer reveals that the first one enjoys simple and affordable steps [168]. In a recent study, Nawaz and colleagues [169], reported the development of a MIP-SPR sensor based on single-layer MIPs for the highly selective, sensitive, and rapid detection of progesterone. To illustrate, the probe was fabricated by using several chain transfer reagents, 2-methyl-2-[(dodecylsulfanylthiocarbonyl)sulfanyl] propanoic acid, which was exploited to photopolymerize a MIP composing of a dicarboxylic acid as a functional monomer (itaconic acid) and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate. The designed sensing platform was able to detect progesterone over a wide concentration range of 1 aM–10 nM. Additionally, the sensor exhibited a low LOD of 0.028 aM, indicating its high sensitivity for progesterone detection. In addition, this platform could detect progesterone in lake water, tap water, and human saliva. In another example, Hubilla et al. [170], introduced a SPR sensor using signal layer functionalized MIP polyterthiophene and histamine for histamine detection. The MIP histamine sensor exhibited a strong linear relationship between the SPR angle shift and the logarithm of histamine concentration, with a R2 value of 0.995. The sensor was capable of detecting histamine concentrations ranging from 15 to 500 μg mL−1, and demonstrated a LOD of 2 μg mL−1. In 2015, Jiang and co-workers [171], designed a MIP-based SPR platform for the quantitative analysis of histamine in aqueous media. In this investigation, the surface of the sensor chip was coated by MIP films through the spin coating method. The analytical performance of the prepared probe was investigated under optimal conditions for the detection of different concentrations of histamine hormone. To elaborate, the change in SPR angle shift with varying concentrations of histamine (ranging from 10 μg L−1 to 2000 μg L−1) was analyzed which revealed a linear relationship between the SPR angle shift and the logarithms of histamine hormone concentrations within the concentration range of 25 μg L−1 to 1000 μg L−1. In another study, Yu et al. [172], fabricated a sensitive platform for the detection of progesterone. The RAFT polymerization mechanism was utilized in the preparation of MIP films using 4-cyano-4 [(dodecylsulfanylthiocarbonyl)sulfanyl]pentanoic acid (CDTPA) as the chain transfer reagent and visible light initiator. The platform prepared to evaluate the analytical performance in PBS with different concentrations of progesterone was examined in the range of 0.1 fM to 1 μM and showed a LOD of 0.1 fM. In addition, there was a linear relationship between the intensity of the reflected light and the concentration, so the reflected light gradually increased with the increase in progesterone concentration.

The use of double-layer MIP has attracted great attention due to its various advantages. Owing to having an additional polymer layer, double-layered MIP prevents the non-specific binding of interfering molecules to the probe surface and this, in turn, increases the sensitivity and selectivity of the sensor. In addition, the additional polymer layer present can improve their stability and make them more suitable for long-term use in sensing applications [173]. In 2015, Tan and colleagues [174], prepared a MIP film with a double-layer structure on the surface of the SPR chip for the sensitive detection of testosterone hormone in aqueous environments. In this work, MIP film was obtained by using in situ UV photopolymerization. Initially, the gold surface of the SPR chip was modified using 1-dodecanethiol. Following, a double-layer molecularly imprinted film was generated on the 1-dodecanethiol-modified gold surface. The LOD of the SPR sensor for testosterone was estimated to be 1 pM. In the same year, Tan and Wei [175], proposed a platform based on double-layer molecularly imprinted film for the highly selective and sensitive detection of E2. 1-dodecanethiol was used to modify the gold surface of the SPR chip. In the next step, double-layer molecularly imprinted film was placed on the previously modified gold surface. A linear correlation was observed between the angle of SPR and the concentrations of E2, over a range spanning 0.25 pM to 2.5 nM. The lowest concentration that could be reliably measured was found to be 0.25 pM.

Several benefits of optical sensors such as good sensitivity and selectivity, non-contact sample measurement and wide dynamic range lead to the introduction of these types of sensors as one of the efficient sensing platforms in favor of hormones detection. On the other hand, these sensing platforms suffer from limitations such as the need for transparent and semi-transparent samples (sample limitation), and alongside that, the interference of other light sources that can affect the accuracy of these sensors.

In the present review study, we conducted a comprehensive investigation into the potential of MIP-based assays for the rapid, reliable, and accurate determination of hormones. Through our investigations, we have found that various types of MIP-based assays have been developed for the detection of hormones including EC and optical detection methods. In our analysis of MIP-based assays for hormone detection, we found that EC voltammetric sensors have been used more than their amperometric and amperometric counterparts. Furthermore, fluorescence sensors are the most commonly developed optical probes for hormones detection. In terms of shortage, it appears that the utilization of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), colorimetric, and UV/Vis-based sensors has been relatively overlooked for quantitative analysis purposes.

To increase the sensitivity of MIP-based sensors for the specific identification of hormones, the use of different nanomaterials can be considered an effective concept. Incorporation of different nanomaterials with MIPs or nano-sized MIPs allows the creation of sensors with good analytical performance. In favor of physicochemical properties, the incorporation of nanomaterials into the polymer matrix significantly enhanced the specific surface area which facilitates the removal of the template from the matrix and provides excellent accessibility to the recognition sites, resulting in high binding capacity and fast binding kinetics. Considering the many advantages of combining MIPs with nanomaterials, we hope that more attention will be paid to them for the emergence of sensors with higher sensitivity, selectivity, and accuracy. After careful examination of the available analytical data for hormone analysis, it is apparent that acceptable analytical results have been achieved using various analytical techniques. To improve the results as much as possible, the use of MIPs in connection with new nanomaterials is recommended. Despite the promising potential of MIPs for hormone analysis, there remain certain limitations and research gaps that need to be addressed in future studies. In conclusion, the demonstration of strengths and weaknesses of developed hormones diagnostic tools can burden researchers’ horizons about the future of this field. In addition, we suggest the incorporation of new advanced nanomaterials, such as layered double hydroxide (LDH) and dendritic fibrous nano-silica (DFNS), in conjunction with MIPs, as a promising approach for the construction of chips capable of simultaneous sensing of multiple hormones.

Another important factor in the reported sensors based on MIPs is portability. Despite the fact that a few portable sensing approaches have been fabricated for hormones quantification, it is expected that these assays will be developed, as simple and on-site tools, in the future. Elaborately, to realize the goal of low-cost, user-friendly and automated hormone detection, the utilization of MIP-based smartphone sensors and portable microchips is highly recommended. One of the basic challenges in the field of hormone measurement by MIP-based sensors is the inability to detect several hormones by one biosensor. In this regard, it is recommended to develop microfluidic assays capable of measuring several hormones at the same time. In conclusion, we strongly recommend the design and development of MIP-based smartphone sensors and microchips that can perform quantitative analysis of multiple hormones simultaneously. Such devices hold great potential for the reliable and accurate detection of hormones in human biofluids, including sweat, tears, blood, urine, and saliva.
